main_summary.summary: Mary Jones, born July 24, 1933 in Georgia and raised on her grandmother Bertha McIntosh's small farm outside Albany, recounts her childhood as a field laborer under Miss Alice Porter, her education at Shepherd Elementary (held inside First Bethel Baptist Church) and Monroe Comprehensive High School, and her marriage to Jimmy Lee Jones in 1951. She describes her grassroots organizing during the Albany Movement: voter-registration canvassing in the Washington Projects, literacy classes preparing residents for the Georgia Constitution test required to register, and service on the police committee that pressed Chief Laurie Pritchett to hire Albany's first Black police officers (Etheridge Smith, David Hyde, Robert Benning Jr., and others). Jones marched with roughly 700 adults on Washington and Broad Avenue, was arrested, and served from Monday to Thursday in Baker County jail before her father bonded her out. She describes the 1961 Black Christmas Boycott of downtown Albany, the Criterion Club leadership of Dr. William G. Anderson, C.B. King, Slater King, Thomas Chapman, and Marion S. Page, the integration ordeal her oldest daughter faced when she signed her into the previously-white high school, and the Head Start program (under Sargent Shriver and the War on Poverty) through which Jones later obtained her own early-childhood education credentials.
key_themes: First Black police officers in Albany
main_summary.historical_significance:
Jones provides a ground-level account of the Albany Movement from a non-clergy, non-SCLC, female organizer's vantage point. Her testimony documents the Criterion Club's role as the pre-existing voter-registration network from which the Albany Movement organized, names the police-committee delegation that negotiated with Chief Laurie Pritchett, and identifies the first five Black Albany police officers by name. Her account of the 1961 Black Christmas Boycott, driving residents to Cordele and other towns to shop after the downtown was cut off, adds operational detail rarely captured in published Albany Movement histories.

Jones also corrects the dominant narrative that SCLC and Dr. King initiated the Albany campaign: she states that 700 adults were already in jail when King arrived, in direct response to an Albany Herald editorial (attributed to publisher James H. Gray) that accused the adult community of pushing children to march in their place. Her parallel account of her oldest daughter's integration of the previously-white high school under principal Mr. McNair documents the daily harassment Black students endured.

chapters.title: Childhood on Grandmother's Farm
chapters.summary: Born July 24, 1933, Mary Jones grew up on the small parcel her grandmother Bertha McIntosh purchased outside Albany. Her grandmother reared her on what the family called a 'slot' of land. The chapter establishes the rural Black agricultural world of southwest Georgia in the 1930s and 40s.

chapters.title: Field Labor Under Miss Alice Porter
chapters.summary: From age eight, Jones worked white-owned farms gathering peanuts, picking cotton, digging potatoes, pulling corn, and gathering pecans. Miss Alice Porter supervised neighborhood children in the fields. Workdays began around seven and ended at five; the children earned about 75 cents per day, saved toward school clothes.

chapters.title: Shepherd Elementary at First Bethel
chapters.summary: Jones attended Shepherd Elementary, held inside First Bethel Baptist Church, one of the oldest Black churches in southwest Georgia, where W.E.B. Du Bois had reportedly visited. The school occupied a former barn given by the white community when they built their own church. Miss Willie Mae Jackson taught grades one through three; Miss Celeste Scott taught grades four through seven.
related_events: segregated Black elementary education in Georgia

chapters.title: Monroe High and Marriage
chapters.summary: Jones moved to Albany to attend Monroe Comprehensive High School, living with her aunt Ella Holloway and three cousins. She played basketball and ran track. She was scheduled to graduate in 1951 but married classmate Jimmy Lee Jones, a Monroe football and basketball player, on the Tuesday after a Friday-night basketball game. He left for the Army the following day.

chapters.title: Daughter's School Integration
chapters.summary: Jones signed her oldest daughter into the second integrated class at Albany's previously-white high school. Principal Mr. McNair called the Black students 'little monkeys.' Students faced spitting, harassment in the cafeteria, and daily threats. Jones describes phoning around to track her daughter's safety in an era before household phones were common. Her daughter persisted because friends had enrolled with her.

chapters.title: Husband's Military Service
chapters.summary: Jones's husband Jimmy Lee served two years of active duty, returned, joined the Reserves, and was called back to active duty during the Albany Movement era. While he was away Jones raised the family, taught the children that they 'were somebody regardless of the color of the skin,' and read the Pittsburgh Courier and A.C. Searles's Southwest Georgian for news of the integration struggle.
related_events: Black press coverage of the civil rights movement

chapters.title: Voter Registration Work
chapters.summary: After Rosa Parks and the Montgomery bus boycott, Jones joined the Criterion Club's voter-registration drive. She canvassed door-to-door in the Washington Projects where she lived, taught literacy classes to help residents pass the Georgia Constitution reading test required for registration, drove people to the courthouse to register, and then drove them back to vote. She describes the persistent challenge of converting registration into actual ballot-casting.

chapters.title: Police Committee and First Black Officers
chapters.summary: Jones served on the seven-member police committee that pressed Chief Laurie Pritchett to hire Black officers. McCree Harris led the delegation. Pritchett initially ignored them through three or four visits. Harris ultimately brought completed applications back to him. Five men were hired: Etheridge Smith, David Hyde, Robert Benning Jr., and two others. The new officers walked beats without patrol cars, wore uniforms, but the committee acted in response to weekly killings of Black men by white officers.
related_events: first Black police officers Albany Georgia 1961

chapters.title: Criterion Club Leadership
chapters.summary: The Criterion Club, the pre-existing Black civic and voter-registration network in Albany, became the organizational core of the Albany Movement. Jones names its leadership: Dr. William G. Anderson (elected president of the Albany Movement), Chevene 'C.B.' King, Slater King, Thomas Chapman, and Marion S. Page (secretary).

chapters.title: Freedom Riders and Mass Meetings
chapters.summary: The Freedom Riders arrived in Albany and were welcomed at the train station before being arrested. Mass meetings followed almost nightly, rotated among Black churches; some ministers feared cooperation because rural churches were being burned. Dr. King helped fundraise to rebuild the burned churches. Jones met King only briefly in receiving lines.

chapters.title: March and Baker County Jail
chapters.summary: After James H. Gray's Albany Herald editorial accused adults of pushing children to march, Jones and roughly 700 adults organized at Washington and Broad Avenue and marched to the old jail. All were arrested. Jones spent Monday through Thursday in Baker County jail; 25 women shared a cell with one bed; food was hard peas, grits with bread thrown on top, and black-eyed peas. The Albany Movement was a local action; she stresses 700 were already jailed when King arrived. Her father bonded her out.

chapters.title: Black Christmas Boycott
chapters.summary: Jones drove people to Cordele and other surrounding towns to shop during the 1961 Black Christmas Boycott of downtown Albany. Residents retrieved their layaway items before December, then refused to shop downtown. The boycott pressured Albany merchants to open jobs to Black workers beyond cleaning and freight-handling roles.

chapters.title: Voting Captain and Project Organizing
chapters.summary: Jones continued grassroots work in the Washington Projects: meetings at her house at noon each day so older residents who couldn't walk far could attend, voting-captain duties identifying preferred candidates and notifying residents who to vote for, and coordination with neighbors who provided childcare while organizers were jailed.

chapters.title: Teachers' Committee and Legacy
chapters.summary: The school superintendent threatened teachers with dismissal if they marched. The teachers' committee instead supported jailed marchers, paying rent, light bills, and buying food for the families. Jones reflects that the Albany Movement succeeded by opening jobs and improving schools; she names prayer, sticking together, and learning to love each other as the central legacies, alongside acknowledgment of the quiet white allies who supported the movement.

chapters.title: Head Start and Education
chapters.summary: Through the War on Poverty under President Lyndon Johnson and Sargent Shriver, Jones entered the Head Start program and pursued her own delayed education in early childhood. She defends Head Start funding from cuts because, she says, her education came from that program.

loc_video.caption: Mary Jones oral history interview conducted by Will Griffin in Albany, Georgia, 2013 March 09
